Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

DEFENDANT LIAM BOOTH'S Joinder In Defendant Limitless Specialty Services Associates' Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Judicial Proceedings.

Defendant Liam Booth's joinder is granted.

The arbitration agreement provides, "You and the Company hereby agree that any dispute between You and the Company (including all of its parent(s), successor(s), predecessor(s)-in-interest, subsidiaries, related and affiliated companies and entities, and each of the foregoing companies' and entities' respective divisions, officers, directors, shareholders, partners, limited partners, joint ventures, agents, investors, employees, representatives, independent contractors, payroll companies, attorneys, insurers, licensees and assigns, past, present or future)." (Emmer Decl., Ex. 2 (emphasis added).)

Plaintiff's sole claim against Defendant Booth depends upon the boilerplate allegations in the underlying complaint that Booth is a "managing agent" of Plaintiff's joint employer, Limitless Specialty Services Associates, LLC ("LSSA"). As such, Plaintiff's allegation of agency is "sufficient to support a motion to compel arbitration even if the allegation is not treated as a judicial admission." (Barsegian v. Kessler & Kessler (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 446, 453 n. 3.)

As an alleged agent of LSSA, Booth fits into the class of intended third-party beneficiaries of the arbitration agreement. (See Cohen v. TNP 2008 Participating Notes Program, LLC (2019) 31 Cal. App. 5th 840, 856; Boucher v. All. Title Co. (2005) 127 Cal.App.4th 262.) Furthermore, Plaintiff's cause of action arises out of or is connected to his employment and is therefore subject to mandatory arbitration. Plaintiff brings a cause of action for sexual harassment under the Fair Employment and Housing Act ("FEHA"), alleging harassing comments and instances of unwanted sexual touching at work-sponsored events and travel. (Complaint, 45-48.) Plaintiff does not bring a cause of action for sexual assault or battery. As such, this matter is more analogous to Bigler v. Harker School (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 727 (2013) than Victoria v. Superior Court (1985) 40 Cal.3d 734.

Finally, Plaintiff's citation to CCP 1281.2(c) [court can deny arbitration where there is a risk of inconsistent rulings] founders. The FAA applies to the parties' agreement, so the adduced rule of the CAA is displaced. (See Mastick v. TD Ameritrade, Inc. (2012) 209 Cal.App.4th 1258, 1263-1264.)

Order is signed.

Notice Of Motion And Motion Seeking Leave To Proceed Anonymously *** Redacted Version ***
Off Calendar Feb-10-2022 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. COLLEEN M. MULLEN, Esq. of Employee Justice Legal Group, P.C. (Tel: 213-382-2222) for plaintiff;
Atty. MARGARET G. MARASCHINO, Esq. of Munger, Tolles & Olson LLP (Tel: 213-683-9100) for defendants Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, MPPR Associates, LLC, Brian Mosteller, Limitless Specialty Services Associates, LLC, CZI Services, LLC, and West Street, LLC;
Atty. PUNEET K. SANDHU, Esq. of Young, Zinn, Pazzani & Sandhu LLP (Tel: 213-362-1860) for defendant Liam Booth.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF JOHN DOE's Motion Seeking Leave To Proceed Anonymously.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to proceed anonymously is granted.

In Does I thru XXIII v. Advanced Textile Corp. (9th Cir.2000) 214 F.3d 1058, 1067, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals noted that federal courts "have permitted plaintiffs to use pseudonyms in three situations: (1) when identification creates a risk of retaliatory physical or mental harm [citations]; (2) when anonymity is necessary 'to preserve privacy in a matter of sensitive and highly personal nature,' [citations]; and (3) when the anonymous party is 'compelled to admit [his or her] intention to engage in illegal conduct, thereby risking criminal prosecution,' [citations]." (Id. at p. 1068.) The court went on to hold that "a party may preserve his or her anonymity in judicial proceedings in special circumstances when the party's need for anonymity outweighs prejudice to the opposing party and the public's interest in knowing the party's identity." (Id.)

In this case, anonymity is proper to protect plaintiff's privacy interest because his/her claims implicate sexual assault and a medical condition. (See Doe v. Bakersfield City Sch. Dist. (2006) 136 Cal.App.4th 556 [permitting plaintiff to proceed anonymously when plaintiff alleged he was sexually abused by a school counselor]; Jane Doe 8015 v. Sup. Ct. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 489 [permitting a plaintiff to proceed anonymously when plaintiff's HIV-status was a critical component of the allegations]; Doe v. Lincoln Unified Sch. Dist. (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 758, 767 [anonymity allowed where teacher brought action that implicated her mental illness].) In Starbucks Corp. v. Superior Court (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1436, 1452, fn. 7, the Court of Appeal noted: "The judicial use of 'Doe plaintiffs' to protect legitimate privacy rights has gained wide currency, particularly given the rapidity and ubiquity of disclosures over the World Wide Web."

Plaintiff's need for anonymity outweighs the prejudice to the defendants and the public's interest in knowing the plaintiff's identity. Plaintiff has a strong interest in keeping his/her medical condition private. His/her medical condition relates to a matter of a sensitive and highly personal nature. The public generally has a strong interest in protecting the identities of sexual assault victims so that other victims will not be deterred from reporting such crimes. The court further notes that defendant has already been subjected to public scrutiny as a result of plaintiff's allegations. Defendant fails to show a waiver even if plaintiff's identity was disclosed in DFEH filings.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order *** Redacted Version ***
Off Calendar Feb-10-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. COLLEEN M. MULLEN, Esq. of Employee Justice Legal Group, P.C. (Tel: 213-382-2222) for plaintiff;
Atty. MARGARET G. MARASCHINO, Esq. of Munger, Tolles & Olson LLP (Tel: 213-683-9100) for defendants Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, MPPR Associates, LLC, Brian Mosteller, Limitless Specialty Services Associates, LLC, CZI Services, LLC, and West Street, LLC;
Atty. PUNEET K. SANDHU, Esq. of Young, Zinn, Pazzani & Sandhu LLP (Tel: 213-362-1860) for defendant Liam Booth.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF JOHN DOE's Motion For Protective Order.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion for a protective or "gag" order is denied.

Such restraints on the speech of litigants are permissible if: "(1) the speech sought to be restrained poses a clear and present danger or serious and imminent threat to a protected competing interest; (2) the order is narrowly tailored to protect that interest; and (3) no less restrictive alternatives are available." (Hurvitz v. Hoefflin (2000) 84 Cal.App.4th 1232, 1241-1242.)

Here, plaintiff fails to meet that heavy burden. Plaintiff's right to privacy does not justify the prior restraint. "[S]paring citizens from embarrassment, shame, or even intrusions into their privacy has never been held to outweigh the guarantees of free speech in our federal and state Constitutions." (Id. at 1244.) The court believes that a generic protective order controlling the confidentiality of exchanged information can adequately protect plaintiff's interests, especially if he/she is allowed to proceed anonymously. Defendants persuasively argue that the requested order is overly broad and dramatically restricts defendants' ability to prepare for trial. "The proposed protective order permits Defendants and their counsel to disclose Plaintiff's true identity to any individual necessary, so long as that individual understands Plaintiff's identity shall be kept confidential." (Mullen Decl., par. 10.) The proposed order requires third parties to execute a non-disclosure agreement before learning of plaintiff's identity and opens those third parties up to contempt sanctions. (Mullen Decl., Ex. D (par. 4).) Defendant's ability to communicate with third party witnesses would be severely hampered if the court issued the requested order. In Hurvitz, 84 Cal.App.4th at 1245, the court found a similar scheme to be "particularly troubling."

The parties' request for sanctions are denied.

Order signed.

The Court informs the parties that if a party in this case files another motion for protective order, the motion may be set for hearing directly on the 9:30 a.m. Law & Motion Calendar (instead of the 9:00 a.m. Discovery Calendar) to be heard by Judge Ulmer.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)

Atty. PUNEET K. SANDHU, Esq. of Young, Zinn, Pazzani & Sandhu LLP (Tel: 213-362-1860) for defendant Liam Booth.

Hearing Re: Motion To File Under Seal Portions Of Plaintiff's Replies ISO Motion For Leave To Proceed Anonymously Motion For A Protective Order.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's unopposed motion to seal is granted. There is good cause to seal the adduced documents, which implicate plaintiff's privacy rights and medical condition. The court finds that: (1) there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) the overriding interest supports sealing the record; (3) a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and (5) no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (Cal. R. Ct., Rule 2.550(d).) Plaintiff shall prepare an order that reflects these findings and specifically cites the sealed portions of the documents.

Counsel for prevailing party to prepare and submit a proposed order.
